data.title: Northern Trust Stock Just Jumped More Than 5%. Here's Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

Northern Trust shares went on a tear Tuesday morning, jumping 5.7%. The catalyst? Surging first-quarter earnings that beat Wall Street forecasts.

The Chicago-based wealth and asset manager reported earnings per share of $2.71, topping analysts' expectations of $2.32, according to estimates compiled by FactSet. For the same period a year ago, Northern Trust posted earnings per share of $1.90.

Northern Trust reported revenue of $2.2 billion, ahead of analyst forecasts for $2.1 billion. For the same period a year ago, revenue came in at $1.9 billion.

Northern Trust shares were changing hands at $167.65, up 5.4% at 11:39 a.m. ET, while the benchmark S&P 500 index was down 0.3%. Early Thursday morning, Northern Trust's stock hit $167.91, which would represent a new closing high.

CEO Michael O'Grady said the company's 14% year-over-year increase in revenue was underpinned by "improved organic growth, elevated client activity across our businesses, and favorable interest-rate and market conditions."

On the company's earnings call, he said Northern Trust is expanding its use of artificial intelligence and enlarging its investment offering for clients, including new alternative investment funds and strategies.

The company's growth came amid a volatile quarter marked by geopolitical turmoil that has weighed on markets. The S&P is up just 3.5% this year. Northern Trust's stock has risen 23% over the same period.

The company has a large asset-management business as well as a wealth management unit that serves ultrawealthy clients and their families. Northern Trust's total assets under custody and administration rose 10% year over year to $18.5 trillion.

In June, The Wall Street Journal reported that Bank of New York Mellon had approached Northern Trust to discuss a potential merger. In July, O'Grady denied the company had any such talks and said Northern Trust was committed to remaining independent.

Write to Andrew Welsch at andrew.welsch@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: LVMH: Share transactions disclosure
data.description:
Paris, April 21st, 2026

The disclosure of share transactions carried out from April 13th to April 17th, 2026, was sent to the AMF on April 21st 2026. As required by current law, this document is publically available and can be consulted on the Company’s website (www.lvmh.com) under the section «regulated information».

LVMH

LVMH Moët Hennessy Louis Vuitton is represented in Wines and Spirits by a portfolio of brands that includes Moët & Chandon, Dom Pérignon, Veuve Clicquot, Krug, Ruinart, Mercier, Château d’Yquem, Domaine du Clos des Lambrays, Château Cheval Blanc, Colgin Cellars, Hennessy, Glenmorangie, Ardbeg, Belvedere, Woodinville, Volcán de mi Tierra, Chandon, Cloudy Bay, Terrazas de los Andes, Cheval des Andes, Bodega Numanthia, Ao Yun, Château d’Esclans, Château Galoupet, Joseph Phelps and Château Minuty. Its Fashion and Leather Goods division includes Louis Vuitton, Christian Dior, Celine, Loewe, Kenzo, Givenchy, Fendi, Emilio Pucci, Marc Jacobs, Berluti, Loro Piana, RIMOWA, Patou, Barton Perreira and Vuarnet. LVMH is present in the Perfumes and Cosmetics sector with Parfums Christian Dior, Guerlain, Parfums Givenchy, Kenzo Parfums, Perfumes Loewe, Benefit Cosmetics, Make Up For Ever, Acqua di Parma, Fresh, Fenty Beauty by Rihanna, Maison Francis Kurkdjian and Officine Universelle Buly. LVMH's Watches and Jewelry division comprises Bulgari, TAG Heuer, Tiffany & Co, Chaumet, Zenith, Fred, Hublot and l’Epée. LVMH is also active in Selective Retailing as well as in other activities through DFS, Sephora, Le Bon Marché, La Samaritaine, Groupe Les Echos-Le Parisien, Paris Match, Cova, Le Jardin d’Acclimatation, Royal Van Lent, Belmond and Cheval Blanc hotels.

data.title: Critical Minerals Shares Fall Following $60M Private Placement
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of Critical Minerals fell after the company said it had raised around $60 million through a private placement.

The stock was down 9.3% to $10.83 as of late Tuesday morning. The stock has risen 56% this year.

The company said it sold about 6 million shares to institutional investors at a price of $10 each.

It said proceeds from the offering will be used for working capital purposes, including development work on its Tanbreez Project.

"This capital accelerates our expansion and positions CRML to emerge as a global leader in critical metals, while strengthening our foundation to create significant value for shareholders and the broader industry," Chief Executive Tony Sage said.

The offering is expected to close on or about Wednesday, the company said.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Intel Stock Gets 2 Upgrades. The Rally Is Back On. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Alex Kozul-Wright

Intel's recent rally reignited Tuesday after one of the few bearish analysts on the stock upgraded the shares.

BNP Paribas analyst David O'Connor upgraded Intel stock from Underperform to Neutral on Monday. He also lifted BNP's price target to $60, from $34 previously. Just five analysts have a Sell or equivalent rating on the shares, out of 49 covering the stock, according to FactSet data.

"Agentic AI is driving very strong demand for server CPU's with hyperscalers scrambling to secure supply," O'Connor said in a research note.

Intel's rally ground to a halt Monday, possibly as investors repositioned themselves ahead of Thursday's earnings report. KeyBanc thinks the chip maker's stock has further to run.

"The real cyclical recovery has yet to begin," KeyBanc analysts, led by John Vinh, wrote in a note Monday. They added that they favor Intel, along with Micron and Nvidia.

They rate Intel stock as Overweight, with a target price of $70. The shares were up 1.5% at $66.70 in early trading. Before Monday's 4.1% fall, Intel stock had risen 11 of the previous 12 trading sessions, dating back to March 31.

"Given the... added durability of AI infra build-out as a sustained demand driver, we believe this recovery can extend at least through 2027," Vinh added.

Intel has been on a tear so far this year--shares are up 82% since the start of January — in large part due to elevated demand for central processing units from AI hyperscalers.

Write to Alex Kozul-Wright at alexander.kozul-wright@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Vicor Shares Touch All-Time High After 1Q Earnings Beat Expectations
data.description:
By Christopher Kuo

Vicor shares gained after the company's first quarter earnings beat analysts' expectations and its backlog surged.

Shares of the Massachusetts-based

The power systems company's stock rose 6.8% to $240.16 in late morning trading on Tuesday. That was down from the an all-time high of $256.37 earlier in the session. The company's shares have been on a tear in the past year posting a five fold increase in price, and are up 33% in the last month.

Vicor posted a first quarter profit of $20.7 million, or 44 cents a share, compared with $2.5 million, or 6 cents a share, a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet had expected earnings of 37 cents a share.

The company recorded revenue of $113 million, beating Wall Street forecasts of $109.4 million. The company said its backlog for the first quarter was $301 million, a 75% increase from the same period a year earlier.

Write to Christopher Kuo at chris.kuo@wsj.com

data.title: LatAm assets mixed as investors mull US-Iran talks; Peru, Romania in focus
data.description:
Key points:
  • Oil prices steady at $95 a barrel as investors weigh potential US-Iran peace talks
  • US, Mexico to launch formal USMCA talks week of May 25, USTR says
  • Peru's election results delayed as authorities review contested ballots
  • Romania's PM Ilie Bolojan says he will lead minority government after coalition split

By Johann M Cherian

Currencies and stocks of most major Latin American economies were mixed on Tuesday as investors weighed the likelihood of potential U.S.-Iran talks to end the Middle East conflict and monitored political developments in Peru and Romania.

MSCI's index tracking Latin American stocks (.MILA00000PUS) slipped 0.2%, while a broader emerging markets equities index CBOE:EFS was on track for a record high close - a first since the Iran-U.S. conflict broke out in late February.

A Latam FX gauge (.MILA00000CUS) inched up 0.4% to hit a record high versus the dollar, with Brazil's real leading gains with a 0.2% rise.

Oil prices, a key driver for emerging markets, held their ground near $96 a barrel as crude shipments through the strategic Strait of Hormuz were uncertain and the U.S. military announced it had boarded a huge Iranian oil tanker at sea in international waters.

A two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran is expected to expire at 8 p.m. ET on Wednesday and talks on a peace deal between the U.S. and Iran are expected to take place this week.

"Our view is that both Iran and the U.S. are looking for a graceful way to de-escalate," said AJ Mediratta, partner at Greylock Capital Management.

"Both sides have made their points. However, the contradictory statements out of Iran suggest that the new leadership in Iran is still coalescing. And it's not unreasonable for the Iranians to be untrusting given how volatile the Trump administration has been. But Iran badly, badly needs that strait to be open."

POLITICAL JITTERS IN PERU, ROMANIA

Uncertainty prevailed over electoral results in Peru over a week ago.

The country's electoral authorities have begun reviewing thousands of contested ballots, delaying final results, as no clear presidential rival has emerged to face conservative frontrunner Keiko Fujimori in a June runoff.

The local stocks index (.MIPE00000PUS) fell 1.6% and the sol slipped 0.1%, while international bonds (US715638DP43=TE), (US715638DF60=TE) were also broadly unchanged.

Elsewhere in emerging Europe, Romanian Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan said he will lead a minority government to ensure that reforms needed to tap European Union funds are met, a day after the leftist Social Democrats (PSD) - his coalition's biggest party - withdrew their support.

The country's currency was flat at 5 per euro, although investors were pricing in selling pressure to weigh in the months ahead, with a one-month leu forward swap (EURRON3M=) jumping 17% to 172 per euro.

The local stocks index fell 1.5% to a two-week low, while the cost of insuring against a potential sovereign default jumped over 1.3 basis points to a two-week high of 157.

Brokerage JPMorgan downgraded the country's debt to 'market weight' from 'equal weight' on Monday.

Meanwhile, markets were keen on the outcome of a potential revamp to the U.S., Mexico and Canada free-trade deal, with the first bilateral round of talks set for the week of May 25.

During his latest visit to Mexico, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer told the country's auto and steel industries that they should not expect the renegotiation to remove tariffs on their sectors, according to sources.

Uncertainty about the outcome of the negotiations is likely to hold back the local economy this year, a Reuters poll of economists showed.

The local peso dipped 0.1% on Tuesday and international bonds were marginally lower, while local stocks lost 1.8%.

Elsewhere, Argentina posted a trade surplus of $2.52 billion in March, more than double what analysts polled by Reuters were anticipating. The peso was steady.
